texts.full_text: at the bottom of the vessel. Thereafter, the liquid which is free from particles is removed by decantation or aspiration. However, removing the liquid from the vessel must be carried out very carefully so as not to remove the particles. Publication EP-140787 (corresponding to publication U.S. Pat. No. 4,649,116) proposes a method by which micro-size magnetic particles are separated from a liquid by using a magnetic rod which is pushed thereto. The particles are pulled off the rod by using a stronger magnet. Publication WO-86/06493 suggests a method to be used in immunoassays, in which an open container holding a sample containing microspheres (which may also include antibodies or antigens) is placed within the magnetic field of a permanent magnet. The spherical particles are drawn towards and collect on one side of the container to their magnetic properties, allowing for easy separation from non-magnetic components in the solution. However, both methods have limitations: EP-140787 requires manual handling which can be time consuming when processing multiple samples simultaneously; WO-86/06493 necessitates a strong permanent magnet that might not always provide optimal separation efficiency or could potentially damage sensitive biomaterials due to high magnetic field strength. Therefore, there is still room for improvement in developing more and methods of separating
